topic: Error Handling - Context Wrapping Without External Crates

turns.content: I just solved the file read problem by manually wrapping my io::Error in a struct and implementing Display to print the path, but I feel like there must be a simpler way that doesn't require me to write all those boilerplate traits myself. It feels so tedious compared to what I've seen in tutorials.

It is frustrating when you have to manually implement traits for something as common as error handling. You are absolutely right, there is a better pattern once you stop thinking of it as writing code and start seeing it as composing logic. Instead of creating your own struct every time, you can use an enum with a Context variant or simply wrap the io::Error directly using map_err to chain the source information automatically without defining a new type each call. For example, if you have a function that returns Result<PathBuf, io::Error>, you can just do something like .map_err(|e| (e.path(), e)). This allows you to manually chain source() and attach context naturally while still using standard library types like std::path::Path and the Error trait without needing external crates like anyhow or thiserror. But wait, before we go deeper into that pattern, could you tell me specifically what error type your current implementation is returning right now? Is it a custom Result<T, E> where E is your manual struct, or are you already using something like std::io::Error? This will determine whether we focus on the enum approach or direct wrapping.
